title: Marble Mania
description: Simulate a circular marble game where every 23rd marble scores points, and find the winning score for Part 1 and with 100× more marbles for Part 2.
writeup:
## Approach

The key insight is choosing the right data structure. A plain list works for Part 1 but is O(n²) — inserting and removing in the middle is slow. A `deque` with rotations makes both operations O(1).

## The Deque Trick

Instead of tracking an explicit "current" index, keep the current marble always at the **right end** of the deque:

- **Normal marble:** rotate left by 1 (moves the insertion point one step clockwise), then `append` the new marble to the right end.
- **Multiple-of-23 marble:** rotate right by 7 (moves 7 steps counter-clockwise), `pop` the marble at the right end (score it + the marble number), then rotate left by 1 to land on the new current.

This means every operation is an O(1) deque rotation or append/pop — no index arithmetic, no list shifting.

## Part 1

Run the simulation for the given last-marble number and return `max(scores)`.

## Part 2

Same simulation, but with `last_marble × 100`. The deque approach handles this in the same time complexity — a list-based solution would be ~10 000× slower here.

## Code Highlights

- `circle.rotate(-1)` / `circle.rotate(7)` replace all index math.
- `scores[marble % num_players]` maps each marble to a player without tracking turns separately.
- Visualizer animates the first 80 turns showing the real circle, then switches to a progress bar for the remainder.
